title: Apply: forces and Newton's third law
description: Apply your knowledge of forces and Newton's third law in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.
topic_path: Apply: forces and Newton's third law

assessment_items.question_content:
A student pushes a milk carton with a force of $1.5\,\text{N}$ to the right to slide it across a lunch table. There are also a leftward friction force of $1.0\,\text{N},$ a downward gravitational force of $2.0\,\text{N},$ and an upward normal force of $2.0\,\text{N}$ acting on the carton.  These forces are modeled below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the statements.**

* The vertical forces acting on the carton are [[☃ dropdown 3]].

* The horizontal forces acting the carton are [[☃ dropdown 4]].

* The net force acting on the carton is [[☃ dropdown 1]] in the [[☃ dropdown 5]] direction.
hints: The net force is the result of combining all individual forces acting on an object. When combining individual forces, their directions matter.
If two forces act in the same direction, we add their strengths. If they act in opposite directions, we subtract their strengths.

The vertical forces on the carton act in opposite directions. So, we subtract their strengths:

Net vertical force = $2.0\,\text{N}-2.0\,\text{N}$

Net vertical force = $0\,\text{N}$

The vertical forces are *balanced*.

The horizontal forces on the carton are in opposite directions. So, we subtract their strengths:

Net horizontal force = $1.5\,\text{N}-1.0\,\text{N}$

Net horizontal force = $0.5\,\text{N}$ to the right

The horizontal forces are *unbalanced*. The larger horizontal force is rightward, so the net force is rightward.

The answer is:

* The vertical forces acting on the carton are balanced.

* The horizontal forces acting on the carton are unbalanced.

* The net force acting on the carton is $0.5\,\text{N}$ in the rightward direction.

options.alt: A milk carton is on a horizontal surface. An arrow labeled 2.0 N points upward from the top of the carton. An equal-length arrow labeled 2.0 N points downward from the bottom of the carton.  A slightly shorter arrow labeled 1.5 N points rightward from the right edge of the carton. An even shorter arrow labeled 1.0 N points leftward from the left edge of the carton.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student pushes a milk carton with a force of $1.5\,\text{N}$ to the right to slide it across a lunch table. There are also a leftward friction force of $1.5\,\text{N},$ a downward gravitational force of $2.0\,\text{N},$ and an upward normal force of $2.0\,\text{N}$ acting on the carton.  These forces are modeled below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the statements.**

* The vertical forces acting on the carton are [[☃ dropdown 3]].

* The horizontal forces acting the carton are [[☃ dropdown 4]].

* The strength of the net force acting on the carton is [[☃ dropdown 5]].
hints: The net force is the result of combining all individual forces acting on an object. When combining individual forces, their directions matter.
If two forces act in the same direction, we add their strengths. If they act in opposite directions, we subtract their strengths.

The vertical forces on the carton act in opposite directions. So, we subtract their strengths:

Net vertical force = $2.0\,\text{N}-2.0\,\text{N}$

Net vertical force = $0\,\text{N}$

The vertical forces are *balanced*.

The horizontal forces on the carton are in opposite directions. So, we subtract their strengths:

Net horizontal force = $1.5\,\text{N}-1.5\,\text{N}$

Net horizontal force = $0\,\text{N}$

The horizontal forces are also *balanced*.

The answer is:

* The vertical forces acting on the carton are balanced.

* The horizontal forces acting on the carton are balanced.

* The strength of the net force acting on the carton is $0\,\text{N}.$

options.alt: A milk carton is on a horizontal surface. An arrow labeled 2.0 N points upward from the top of the carton. An equal-length arrow labeled 2.0 N points downward from the bottom of the carton.  A slightly shorter arrow labeled 1.5 N points rightward from the right edge of the carton. An equal-length arrow labeled 1.5 N points leftward from the left edge of the carton.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student pushes a milk carton with a force of $2.5\,\text{N}$ to the left to slide it across a lunch table. There are also a rightward friction force of $1.0\,\text{N},$ a downward gravitational force of $2.0\,\text{N},$ and an upward normal force of $2.0\,\text{N}$ acting on the carton.  These forces are modeled below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the statements.**

* The vertical forces acting on the carton are [[☃ dropdown 3]].

* The horizontal forces acting the carton are [[☃ dropdown 4]].

* The net force acting on the carton is [[☃ dropdown 5]] in the [[☃ dropdown 6]] direction.
hints: The net force is the result of combining all individual forces acting on an object. When combining individual forces, their directions matter.
If two forces act in the same direction, we add their strengths. If they act in opposite directions, we subtract their strengths.

The vertical forces on the carton act in opposite directions. So, we subtract their strengths:

Net vertical force = $2.0\,\text{N}-2.0\,\text{N}$

Net vertical force = $0\,\text{N}$

The vertical forces are *balanced*.

The horizontal forces on the carton are in opposite directions. So, we subtract their strengths:

Net horizontal force = $2.5\,\text{N}-1.0\,\text{N}$

Net horizontal force = $1.5\,\text{N}$ to the left

The horizontal forces are *unbalanced*. The larger horizontal force is leftward, so the net force is leftward.

The answer is:

* The vertical forces acting on the carton are balanced.

* The horizontal forces acting on the carton are unbalanced.

* The net force acting on the carton is $1.5\,\text{N}$ in the leftward direction.

options.alt: A milk carton is on a horizontal surface. An arrow labeled 2.0 N points upward from the top of the carton. An equal-length arrow labeled 2.0 N points downward from the bottom of the carton.  A slightly longer arrow labeled 2.5 N points leftward from the left edge of the carton. A shorter arrow labeled 1.0 N points rightward from the right edge of the carton.

assessment_items.question_content:
A Newton's third law force pair exists whenever two objects interact. The photo below shows a soccer ball being kicked.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Which two forces below make up a Newton's third law force pair?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Every force exerted on an object is one half of a Newton's third law force pair. When a force is exerted on the one object by a second, there’s an equal and opposite force exerted on the second object by the first. These two forces are the two halves of the force pair.

The normal force is the upward force exerted *by the ground on the ball*. So, the other half of this force pair is the downward force exerted *by the ball on the ground*. This force isn’t listed. So, the normal force exerted by the ground on the ball can’t be part of the force pair.

The force of gravity is the downward force exerted *by the Earth on the ball*. So, the other half of this force pair is the upward force of gravity exerted *by the ball on the Earth*. This force isn’t listed. So, the force of gravity exerted by the Earth on the ball can’t be part of the force pair.

The applied force exerted *by the foot on the ball* is one half of the interaction between the foot and the ball. So, the other half of this force pair is the applied force exerted *by the ball on the foot*. This force is also listed in the options.

The two forces that make up a Newton's third law force pair are:

* the applied force exerted by the foot on the ball
* the applied force exerted by the ball on the foot

options.alt: A soccer ball on the ground is being kicked to the right by a boy.

choices.content: the normal force exerted by the ground on the ball
the force of gravity exerted by the Earth on the ball
the applied force exerted by the foot on the ball
the applied force exerted by the ball on the foot

assessment_items.question_content:
A Newton's third law force pair exists whenever two objects interact. The photo below shows a roller coaster coasting downhill.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which two forces below make up a Newton's third law force pair?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Every force exerted on an object is one half of a Newton's third law force pair. When a force is exerted on the one object by a second, there’s an equal and opposite force exerted on the second object by the first. These two forces are the two halves of the force pair.

The downward force of gravity *by the Earth on the coaster* is one half of the interaction between the Earth and the coaster. So, the other half of this force pair is the upward force of gravity exerted *by the coaster on the Earth*. This force isn’t listed. So, the force of gravity exerted by the Earth on the coaster can’t be part of the force pair.

The friction force exerted by the track on the coaster is one half of the *frictional* interaction between the track and the coaster. 

So, the other half of this force pair is the *friction* force exerted by the coaster on the track. This force isn’t listed. So, the friction force exerted by the track on the coaster can’t be part of the force pair.

The *normal force* exerted by the track on the coaster is one half of the perpendicular interaction between the track and the coaster. So, the other half of this force pair is the *normal force* exerted by the coaster on the track. This force is also listed in the options.

The two forces that make up a Newton's third law force pair are:

* the normal force exerted by the track on the coaster
* the normal force exerted by the coaster on the track

options.alt: A roller coaster coasts down a hill.

choices.content: the normal force exerted by the track on the coaster
the normal force exerted by the coaster on the track
the friction force exerted by the track on the coaster
the force of gravity exerted by the Earth on the coaster

assessment_items.question_content:
A Newton's third law force pair exists whenever two objects interact. The photo below shows an athlete flipping over a tire.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Which two forces below make up a Newton's third law force pair?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Every force exerted on an object is one half of a Newton's third law force pair. When a force is exerted on the one object by a second, there’s an equal and opposite force exerted on the second object by the first. These two forces are the two halves of the force pair.

The upward normal force exerted *by the floor on the tire* is one half of the interaction between the floor and the tire. So, the other half of this force pair is the downward normal force exerted *by the tire on the floor*. This force isn’t listed. So, the normal force exerted by the floor on the tire can’t be part of the force pair.

The downward force of gravity *by the Earth on the tire* is one half of the interaction between the Earth and the tire. So, the other half of this force pair is the upward force of gravity exerted *by the tire on the Earth*. This force isn’t listed. So, the force of gravity exerted by the Earth on the tire can’t be part of the force pair.

The applied force exerted *by the man on the tire* is one half of the interaction between the man and the tire. So, the other half of this force pair is the applied force exerted *by the tire on the man*. This force is also listed in the options.

The two forces that make up a Newton's third law force pair are:

* the applied force exerted by the man on the tire
* the applied force exerted by the tire on the man

options.alt: A man pushes up and to the right on a large tire, which is tilted toward the man.

choices.content: the normal force exerted by the floor on the  tire
the applied force exerted by the man on the tire
the applied force exerted by the tire on the man
the force of gravity exerted by the Earth on the tire

assessment_items.question_content:
A sled is pulled across the ground by a rope. The forces on the sled are modeled below:

[[☃ image 3]]

The force of gravity exerted by the Earth on the sled has a strength of $12\,\text{N}.$

**According to Newton’s third law, which force below must also have a strength of $12\,\text{N}?$**  
*The forces in the diagram are not necessarily shown to scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Newton’s third law states that every force is one half of a simultaneous force pair. Each pair of forces is caused by two objects interacting. One object exerts a force on the other, and the second object exerts a force back on the first.

The two forces that make up a force pair always have equal strengths and point in opposite directions. We’re told the force of gravity exerted by the *Earth* on the *sled* has a strength of $12\,\text{N}.$

So, the force that must also have a strength of $12\,\text{N}$ is the other half of this simultaneous force pair. The other half of the pair is:  

the force of gravity exerted by the *sled* on the *Earth*

options.alt: A sled is on horizontal, snowy ground. A rope is tied to its front, the right edge of the sled, and points up and to the right. An arrow points along this rope. It is labeled, pulling force. A downward arrow, labeled force of gravity and an upward arrow, labeled normal force, have the same length. A fourth arrow points backward, parallel to the ground. It is labeled friction.

choices.content: the normal force exerted by the ground on the sled
the pulling force exerted by the rope on the sled
the friction force exerted by the ground on the sled
the force of gravity exerted by the sled on the Earth

assessment_items.question_content:
A magnet exerts a rightward force of $0.50\,\text{N}$ on a nail. The nail also experiences an upward normal force of $0.03\,\text{N},$ a downward force of gravity of $0.03\,\text{N},$ and a leftward friction force of $0.01\,\text{N},$ as modeled below:

[[☃ image 3]]

**What is the strength of the magnetic force that the nail exerts on the magnet?** 
*The forces in the diagram are not shown to scale.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{N}$
hints: Newton’s third law states that every force is one half of a simultaneous force pair. Each pair of forces is caused by two objects interacting. One object exerts a force on the other, and the second object exerts a force back on the first.

The two forces that make up a force pair always have equal strengths and point in opposite directions. We're asked to find the strength of the magnetic force that the *nail* exerts on the *magnet.* The other half of this force pair is the magnetic force that the *magnet* exerts on the *nail*. We're told that force has a strength of $0.50\,\text{N}.$
So, the strength of the magnetic force that the nail exerts on the magnet is $\boxed{0.50\,\text{N}}.$

options.alt: A nail is laying on a horizontal surface. A hand holds a bar magnet to the right of the  nail. Four arrows are shown pointing outward from the nail.  One arrow points up and is labeled, normal force, 0.03 N. A second arrow points straight down and is labeled, force of gravity, 0.03 N. A third arrow points left. It is labeled, friction force, 0.01 N. A fourth arrow points right. It is labeled, magnetic force, 0.50 N.

assessment_items.question_content:
A car is starting to pull a camper up a hill. The car exerts an applied force of $2{,}100\,\text{N}$ on the camper. The camper also experiences a normal force of $4{,}300\,\text{N},$ a force of gravity of $4{,}500\,\text{N},$ and a friction force of $800\,\text{N},$ as modeled below:

[[☃ image 3]]

**What is the strength of the applied force that the camper exerts backward on the car?**  
*The forces in the diagram are not shown to scale. Enter your answer to the nearest $100\,\text{N}.$*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{N}$
hints: Newton’s third law states that every force is one half of a simultaneous force pair. Each pair of forces is caused by two objects interacting. One object exerts a force on the other, and the second object exerts a force back on the first.

The two forces that make up a force pair always have equal strengths and point in opposite directions. We're asked to find the strength of the force that the *camper* exerts backward on the *car.* The other half of this force pair is the force that the *car* exerts forward on the *camper*. We're told this force has a strength of $2{,}100\,\text{N}.$
So, the strength of the applied force that the camper exerts backward on the car is $\boxed{2{,}100\,\text{N}}.$

options.alt:
A road is inclined slightly up and to the right. A camper and the back half of a jeep is shown on the road. An arrow points from the camper's point of attachment to the jeep, straight up the hill, parallel to the hill. It is labeled, applied force, 2100 N.
A second arrow points straight down from the camper. It is labeled, force of gravity, 4500 N. A third arrow points up and to the left, perpendicular to the road. It is labeled, normal force, 4300 N. A fourth arrow points from the back of the camper downhill, parallel to the hill.  It is labeled, friction force, 800 N.
